coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent paper indicator for seafood freshness monitoring using gas-sensitive carbon dots-based paper indicator
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

Yang, Zhen-quan
Xiao, Lixia
Xu, Xuechao
Guan, Tianzu
Zhou, Wenyuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: A dual-mode gas-sensitive paper indicator was developed by using carbon dots (CDs) as sensing element, focusing on non-contact monitoring of seafood freshness. The CDs was useful for determining ammonia in a linear range of 1.0–100.0 μM. Importantly, the CDs responded to pH changes instantly via both visible colorimetric and fluorescence observation. A pronounced color variation of CDs in both visible colorimetric (from blue to pink) and fluorescent (diminished blue emission) mode was observed concurrently as pH varied from acidity to alkalinity. A paper indicator for pH visualization by naked eyes was prepared by using easy-to-apply drop-coating deposition of CDs solution onto filter paper. Specifically, the feasibility and efficacy of the CDs-based paper indicator was evaluated as a real time non-contact freshness indicator of shrimp, fish and razor clam samples during its storage. The CDs-based color-changing smart indicator reported here provided a basis to develop an efficient dual-mode pH meter for visually assessing seafood freshness in supply chain.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode
Paper indicator
PH sensing
Carbon dots
Seafood freshness

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Color Stability of CDs-based paper indicator
Application of CDs indicator for seafood packaging
Color response to pH and mechanism study
Color response of CDs paper-based indicator
Sensitivity to acid and ammonia vapors
Color stability and reproducibility of the CDs-based paper indicator
Application of the CDs-based paper indicator for seafood packaging

W.Z contributed to methodology, formal analysis and writing original manuscript. L.X. and Z.Y. contributed to methodology and writing\u2013review & editing. X.X. contributed to methodology and formal analysis. T.G. W.Z. and X.G. contributed to methodology and formal analysis. Q.H. contributed to funding acquisition, supervision, methodology, formal analysis and writing\u2013review & editing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The HRTEM image of the as-prepared CDs. The insert is the corresponding particle size distribution histogram. (B) The UV–vis absorption (black line), the excitation (blue line) and the emission (red line) spectra of CDs. The inset shows the image of a CDs solution under UV light (365 nm). (C) The 3D fluorescence emission spectra of CD. (D) The emission spectra of CDs under excitation wavelengths varying from 310 to 420 nm.
(A) Photograph of CDs exposed to different buffer solutions under daylight and (C) UV light. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra and (D) the fluorescence spectra of CDs at different pHs (2.0–12.0).

(A) The zeta potentials of CDs at different pHs (2.0–10.0). (B) The fluorescence spectra of CDs after mixing with various contents of NH3. (C) A plot of the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F/F0) of CDs versus NH3 concentration.

The photographs under (A) visible light and (B) a 365 nm UV illumination of the as-prepared paper indicator integrated with CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) in different buffer solutions (pH 2.0–12.0). (C) Color parameters of the paper indicator prepared with CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) at different pHs (2.0–12.0). (D) The sensitivity of the CDs-based indicator toward ammonia and acid vapors.
Shrimp, fish and razor clam freshness monitoring by the CDs-based indicator under daylight and UV light.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs-based paper indicator and its application for shrimp freshness monitoring.

The color values of the as-prepared paper indicator and the pH and TVB-N values of (A) shrimp, (B) fish and (C) razor clam samples at different time periods after storage. The data were presented as a mean ± SD.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent paper indicator for seafood freshness monitoring using gas-sensitive carbon dots-based paper indicator

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-mode gas-sensitive paper indicator was developed by using carbon dots (CDs) as sensing element, focusing on non-contact monitoring of seafood freshness. The CDs was useful for determining ammonia in a linear range of 1.0–100.0 μM. Importantly, the CDs responded to pH changes instantly via both visible colorimetric and fluorescence observation. A pronounced color variation of CDs in both visible colorimetric (from blue to pink) and fluorescent (diminished blue emission) mode was observed concurrently as pH varied from acidity to alkalinity. A paper indicator for pH visualization by naked eyes was prepared by using easy-to-apply drop-coating deposition of CDs solution onto filter paper. Specifically, the feasibility and efficacy of the CDs-based paper indicator was evaluated as a real time non-contact freshness indicator of shrimp, fish and razor clam samples during its storage. The CDs-based color-changing smart indicator reported here provided a basis to develop an efficient dual-mode pH meter for visually assessing seafood freshness in supply chain.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Seafood has been broadly accepted as an outstanding source of nutritional meat due to the rich sources of minerals, trace elements, fat-soluble vitamins, essential fatty acids and high-quality proteins (Kumar et al., 2020). In recent years, seafood farming is rising rapidly to meet the worldwide demand. However, seafood is easily prone to spoilage because of their high levels of proteins, neutral pH and elevated water activity, which provides favorable medium for microbial growth. Specifically, even under cold storage conditions, the psychrophilic spoilage microorganisms would cause rapid spoilage of seafood (Mohammadalinejhad et al., 2020). Bacterial actions produce large amounts of volatile alkaline compounds such as dimethylamine, trimethylamine, ammonia, amines and etc. (Ahmed et al., 2018; Mohammadalinejhad et al., 2020), which are well known as biogenic amines (BAs). When consumed in excess, physiological side effects such as headache, stomach pain, and vomiting, gastroenteritis, septicemia may appear, and it could even cause adverse carcinogenic effects (Siripongpreda et al., 2020). As such, rapid monitoring of seafood freshness can bring considerable benefits to seafood industry and consumer satisfaction.

As main spoilage indicators of protein-rich meat and seafood, detecting BAs contents was traditionally used to evaluate seafood freshness. The total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) concentration and pH are two highly correlated meat quality indices. Although accurate, conventional analytical methods for TVB-N detection such as gas/liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay usually suffered from lengthy process and complex instrumentation (Ding et al., 2020). Fortunately, the development of various probes to monitor seafood freshness in the last decade has been demonstrated to be a useful substitute to conventional methods. Of diversified probes, pH-responsive paper indicators with non-contact gas sensing ability exhibited promising prospects in seafood freshness monitoring. They were reported to having appealing characteristics of compactness, high reactivity, devoid of expensive instruments, noticeable color change as well as prominent non-contact detectability (Alizadeh-Sani et al., 2020). Even though various articles were reported earlier, nearly all the traditional pH indicators employed synthetic chemical dyes such as bromocresol purple, bromothymol blue, bromocresol green, methyl red, cresol red, xylenol, etc. (Roy, Rhim, 2021). These chemical dyes are toxic and even have carcinogenic effects on human beings. As such, it is highly necessary to develop an efficient, safe and eco-friendly intelligent indicator to replace synthetic dyes.

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging category of nanoscale carbon nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm (Ezati, Priyadarshi, et al., 2022). The CDs are superior due to their acing chemical and physical capabilities such as low toxicity, favorable water solubility, tunable emission and high photo-stability (Zhang et al., 2022). Given these benefits, CD applications in diversified fields such as biological imaging, solar cells, drug delivery, and photovoltaic devices have been rapidly increasing (Ezati, Rhim, et al., 2022). Specifically, recent studies showed that the CDs could serve as a potential crosslinker and activator to fabricate pH-responsive colorant doping indicator systems for freshness monitoring of perishable seafood with non-contact gas sensing ability due to their responses to acid and ammonia vapors. The CDs-based gas-sensitive paper indicator can be prepared by easily and simply drop-coating of CDs solution onto filter paper. The mechanism was based on that the mixture of colorants and CDs responded to color change as pH varied (Ezati, Priyadarshi, et al., 2022). For example, Kilic et al. (Kilic et al., 2022) incorporated red cabbage anthocyanin extract and CDs into gelatin films and realized real-time monitoring of food freshness based on color change, where anthocyanin was the main substance responsible for color change of the proposed gelatin film at different pH values. Ezati et al. (Ezati et al., 2023a) prepared a freshness indicator by using citrus peel-derived CDs integrated with resazurin, where the color change of the proposed indicator in response to pH was due to resazurin. Importantly, recent advances in fabricating pH-responsive CDs with dual-mode color-changing property attracted much attention, benefiting from which a system with both visible colorimetric and fluorescence sensing capability can be realized with CDs as the sole probe; no other colorants were needed. For example, Zhou et al. (Zhou et al., 2023) reported a colorimetric and fluorescent dual‑mode CDs probe for sensing aqueous pH variations. Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2018) developed a pH-sensitive colorimetric and fluorescent dual‑mode sensor, which realized the detection of pH and Cu2 + ions in aqueous phase. Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2020) reported a CDs with pH-responsive functionality for colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode detection of pH in living cells. Although these studies mainly focused on aqueous and/or intracellular pH sensing, they also gave us an inspiration, that is, further exploring the application scope of pH-responsive CDs by fabricating it into gas sensitive indicator so that more promising practical usage in non-contact food freshness monitoring can be achieved.

This work aimed to develop a dual-mode gas-sensitive indicator by using filter paper incorporated with pH-dependent CDs, with a focus on evaluating its potential application for real-time non-contact monitoring of seafood freshness ( Scheme 1). The CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and Trp as precursors. Upon pH stimulation from acidity to alkalinity, the color of CDs varied from blue to pink with similarity to an acid–base indicator. Meanwhile, the florescence emission intensity of CDs witnessed a gradually decreasing trend. With this information, a dual-mode sensing platform with colorimetric and fluorescent sensing functionality was proposed. By simply drop-coating of CDs solution onto filter paper, a CDs-based paper indicator was developed. The sensing mechanism was discussed in detail through detail characterizations. The as-fabricated CDs-based paper strips was finally used for freshness monitoring of shrimp, fish and razor clam samples as a visual spoilage indicator that responded to pH changes. As far as we aware, this is the first report on developing a dual-mode gas-sensitive paper indicator for non-contact seafood freshness monitoring by using CDs as probe, which provided features of rapidity, low cost, straightforward detectability and simple operation principle with no need of sophisticated equipment, which would certainly pave a meaningful pathway for functional CDs applications.

The tryptophan (Trp) was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., LTD (Shanghai, China). The OPD, acetic acid (HAc), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), boric acid (H3BO3), anhydrous ethanol, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water was purified through a Millipore-Q system (Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout the experiments. All chemicals and reagents are of analytical grade or above and do not require further purification prior to use. The Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions (pH 2.012.0) were prepared by blending a mixture solution of 0.04 mol L−1 H3PO4, H3BO3 and HAc with a NaOH solution (0.02 mol L−1).

The CDs was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method according to a previously reported method with slight modification (Zhou et al., 2023). In brief, 0.375 g OPD and 0.528 g Trp were dissolved in 20.0 mL anhydrous ethanol. Then, 1.0 mL of 38 % HCl and 4.0 mL of ultrapure water were added to mix. The mixture solution was then subjected to microwave sonication to ensure complete dissolution. Afterwards, the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and a hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the product was cooled down to room temperature, and the obtained solution containing CDs was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. After removing the ethanol residue by using N2 blowing, the reaction product was dissolved with ultrapure water and finally freeze-dried to get fine powder for further use.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was acquired by using a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurement was carried out on a Varian 670-IR + 610-IR FTIR spectrometer (Lincolnshire, IL, USA). The x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on an ESCALAB 250 x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The elemental analysis of CDs was conducted on a vario EL cube organic elemental analyzer (Elementar, Hanau, Germany). The ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) spectrum was captured by a UV-2550 absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). All the fluorescence data were achieved on a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan).

To develop the paper indicators, the cellulose filter paper (Cytiva., Ltd., Hangzhou, China) was used as indicator carrier and drop coating deposition method was applied according to a previously reported method with suitable modification (Ezati et al., 2023b). A CDs solution (1.0 mg mL−1) was prepared in ultra-pure water as indicator solution, and it was homogenized by ultra-sonication for 10.0 min. The filter paper was cut into pieces of 3.0 × 3.0 cm2. Afterwards, 1.0 mL of the as-prepared CDs indicator solution was added drop-wisely to each piece of paper. After the papers dried naturally in air, 1.0 mL of the CDs indicator solution was added again and dried overnight at room temperature. Finally, the paper-based tags were stored in a dry storage device for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color Stability of CDs-based paper indicator
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the color stability of the CDs-based paper indicator, the as-prepared indicator was sealed in a low-density polyethylene (LDPE) bag and held for one month at room temperature (25 °C). During storage, the color variations were examined by acquiring the color characteristics (L*, a* and b*) of the paper indicator in colorimetric mode by using a chroma meter (Model NR10QC, 3nh, Guangdong, China). The L represents brightness and darkness, a represents the degree of red and green, positive values indicate reddish tint, and negative values indicate greenish tint, and b represents the degree of yellow and blue, positive values indicate yellowish tint, and negative values indicate bluish tint. The total color difference (ΔE) was determined using the following equation:
(1) ΔE = [(ΔL*)2 + (Δa*)2 + (Δb*)2]1∕2
where ΔL*, Δb* and Δa* are the differences between the original color values and the color values after storage at different time intervals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs indicator for seafood packaging
ce:section.ce:para: A glass culture plate (diameter 9 cm, height 3 cm) was taken as storage box, and a piece of CDs-based paper indicator was attached to the inside lid of the plate. A schematic illustration of the placement of the proposed indicator was depicted in Scheme 1. After sterilized by UV light, 45.0 g fresh shrimp, fish and razor clam samples was separately introduced into the glass container, and the lid of the container was sealed. The seafood samples were then stored at 25 ℃ for over a course of 36 h. Three replicates were carried out to monitor the color response of the attached CDs-based indicator at different time intervals.

The TVB-N content was determined by a micro-diffusion method (National Health and Family Planning Commission of the People’s Republic of China, 2016). An alkaline solution supplied by a saturated solution of potassium carbonate (1.0 g mL−1) was used to release TVB-N and the temperature was maintained at 37℃. Then, the volatilized TVB-N was absorbed by boric acid (20.0 g L−1) and titrated with sulfuric acid (0.01 mol L−1) to calculate the TVB-N content. The TVB-N concentration values were expressed in mg/100 g sample (mg%). The pH values of the above-mentioned seafood samples were determined according to a previously published work (Khan et al., 2023). In brief, each sample was firstly chopped. Then, 5.0 g of each slurry sample was weighed and mixed with 25.0 mL of distilled water by vortex in order to be homogeneous. After soaking for 30.0 min, the mixture was filtered, and the pH of the filtrate was measured using a digital pH meter.

The morphology and size information of the as-prepared CDs were determined by HRTEM. Fig. 1A showed a HRTEM image of CDs. The particles were found to be spherical in shape and distributed in a size range of 3.73–11.18 nm with no aggregation was observed. The inset in Fig. 1A was the particle size distribution histogram of CDs assessed by examining the particles at different locations on HRTEM images. By fitting the histogram to a Gaussian model, an average diameter of CDs was found to be 7.32 ± 1.55 nm.

The chemical composition of the CDs was studied by elemental analysis, FTIR and XPS. As shown in Table S1, the CDs was consisted of C 52.87 %, H 4.74 %, N 10.57 %, and O 31.82 %. As displayed by the full scan XPS spectrum of CDs (Fig. S1A), three main predominant peaks at 532.08, 400.08 and 284.08 eV that were ascribed to C1s, N1s and O1s were observed, respectively. Four peaks can be identified in the high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. S1B) with binding energies of 284.70, 284.92, 286.53 and 289.25 eV for CC/CC, CO, CN/CO and COOH, respectively. The XPS of N1s (Fig. S1C) can be resolved into three characteristic peaks of pyridinic N (399.69 eV), amino N (400.66 eV) and graphite N (401.69 eV), respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S1D) showed characteristic peaks at 531.66 and 532.89 eV, corresponding to the presence of CO and CO groups, separately. The FTIR spectrum of CDs was displayed in Fig. S2. The broad absorption band at 3145 −3480 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibrations of O−H and N − H. The absorption peak at 2883 −3035 cm−1 was related to C−H stretching vibration. The sharp absorption band at 1743 cm−1 was correlated with the stretching vibration of CO. The absorption peaks at 1459 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of CN. The C−O tensile vibration induced an absorption peak at 1014 cm−1. The results were in good consistency with XPS analysis. The presence of hydrophilic N − H and O−H groups on CDs surface afforded its good solubility in aqueous phase, which is a prerequisite for the subsequent sensing work.

The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectrum of CDs (black line, Fig. 1B) showed two prominent absorption peaks. The absorption peak at 617 nm was ascribed to n–π* electronic transitions of CO/–N = N– bond and another weak absorption band at 575 nm was assigned to n–π* electronic transitions of C–O/OC–O or C–N/CN bond (Zhou et al., 2023). The fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs (red line, Fig. 1B) revealed a fluorescence emission band at 439 nm under an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs (blue line, Fig. 1B) showed that the CDs had an excitation peak at 370 nm with a large Stokes shift of 69 nm. The 3D fluorescence plot of CDs showed strong fluorescence in a small range of 360–380 nm (Fig. 1C). As illustrated by the inset in Fig. 1B, a CDs aqueous solution emitted brilliant blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV irradiation. The fluorescence properties of CDs at different excitation wavelengths were also studied. As displayed in Fig. 1D, when the excitation wavelength changed from 310 to 420 nm, the emission bands of CDs shifted in a range of 432–456 nm. The typical excitation-dependent emission behavior was believed to be caused by size effect and various functionalities on CDs surface (H. Wang et al., 2019). Using quinine sulfate as a reference standard, the quantum yield of CDs was determined to be 2.54 % (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color response to pH and mechanism study
ce:section.ce:para: The photographs of CDs exposed to different pH solutions were taken at room temperature under daylight and UV illumination ( Fig. 2). As illustrated by the inset in Fig. 2A, the color of CDs solution was noticeably observed by naked eyes changing from blue to bluish violet when pH increased from 2.0 to 6.0 and appeared to be pink when pH was raised to 7.0–12.0. Under UV light, a bright blue luminescence of CDs was found at pH 2.0 (Fig. 2C). As the system shifted from acidic state to alkaline state, the hue of blue emission gradually diminished. The vivid color change of CDs in both daylight and UV light made them an excellent candidate as indicator for monitoring food spoilage.

To study the pH-responsive mechanism of CDs, the fluorescence and absorbance curves of CDs in different pH environment were acquired. It can be seen from Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was strikingly influenced by pH. As pH rose from 2.0 to 12.0, the fluorescence intensity gradually deduced. As displayed by Fig. 2B, the visible absorption of CDs shifts apparently from longer wavelength to the shorter wavelength when the pH was raised from 2.0 to 12.0. Overall, the UV–vis spectra of CDs exposed to different pH conditions had two main absorption regions (550−650 nm and 420−550 nm) with three absorption bands centered around 475, 560, and 610 nm, separately. Notably, the two absorption peaks in the longer wavelength region (∼560 and ∼610 nm) gradually diminished as the acidity lowered and nearly disappeared at pH 8.0, while the absorption peak in shorter wavelength region (∼475 nm) emerged obviously with the increasing of alkalinity. Besides, the two absorption peaks at around 560 and 610 nm showed obvious red shift and the absorption peak at around 510 nm showed certain blue shift as pH enhanced in low pH range (2.0−5.0).

Since the surface of CDs are rich in amine, carboxylic groups, it was deduced that the pH-responsive behavior of CDs optical intensities was most probably due to the protonation and deprotonation of different oxygenated and amino functionalities on CDs surface (Meierhofer et al., 2020). To verify our inference, the zeta potentials of CDs were acquired at various pH values ( Fig. 3 A) given the reason that the surface charge states played a crucial role in governing the UV–vis and fluorescence properties of CDs (Hu et al., 2013). At pH 2.0–3.0, the CDs species were positively charged owing to the protonation of the surface amine groups. An increase in pH caused the reversal of the charge states of CDs surface. When pH was at 4.0–10.0, the CDs species were negatively charged due to the dissociation of carboxylic acid groups. The higher the pH, the more negative the zeta potential of CDs, suggesting the possible change in surface charge states of CDs. To further demonstrate this assumption, the electropherograms of CDs at various pH conditions were acquired given the reason that the CDs were a complex mixture with each species having their unique functional properties (Hu, Meng, and Chan, 2016; Hu, Meng, Choi, et al., 2016) As shown by Fig. S4, the CDs were a complex mixture composing of CDs species in different valence states. At pH 2.0, the CDs species was mostly present as positively charged components. With the increasing of pH (3.0–11.0), the number of positively charged species was gradually reduced while the number of negatively charged peaks increased. Specifically, only negatively charged peaks were observed when pH was increased to 12.0, implying that all the CDs species converted to negatively charged species. The results were in consistence with that from zeta potential measurements. Overall, the above information demonstrated that the pH played a key role in governing the charge states of CDs, which govern the optical properties of CDs.

The sensing capability of CDs was also studied by investigating its fluorescence response to different ammonia (NH3) concentration. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was gradually quenched as the concentration of NH3 linearly increased from 1.0 to 100.0 µM, indicating that the CDs can provide colorimetric sensing of NH3 in a wide concentration range. The calibration curve (Fig. 3C) was plotted between the quenching efficiency (F/F0) and NH3 concentration, where F0 and F are the fluorescent intensity of CDs before and after mixing with NH3. A good linear line relationship was found with a regression equation as follows:
(2) F/F0 = −0.0037x+ 1.2288

The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.81 µM. Table S2 illustrated the analytical performance comparison between the proposed probe and the other CDs-based visual probes for NH3 detection. The proposed method not only achieved a lower LOD (higher sensitivity) than the other CDs-based visual probes. Additionally, compared to the reported single-mode colorimetric or fluorescence CDs-based probe, this work provided dual-mode sensing capability. In essence, the above results revealed the suitability of using proposed CDs probe for NH3 sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color response of CDs paper-based indicator
ce:section.ce:para: The color properties of the CDs-based indicator were studied. As shown by Fig. 4, both the visible color and the fluorescence emission color of CDs-based indicator were pH-responsive with reasonable color shifts were observed. Under daylight (Fig. 4A), the color of the CDs-based indicator was blue at pH 2.0. It gradually changed to bluish violet when pH was raised from 2.0 to 6.0 and then turned to be pink at pH 7.0. At pH 8.0–12.0, the pink color became more intense. Under UV light (Fig. 4B), the color of CDs-based indicator showed bright blue emission at pH 2.0. As pH increased, the hue of blue emission gradually diminished and the proposed indicator turned to be bluish violet as the pH was improved to pH 5.0–8.0, and it finally appeared to be pink hue when pH was above 8.0. The results clearly indicated that the CDs were successfully immobilized on the paper matrix.

These color changes were quantitatively confirmed by determining significant color values such as L-, a-, and b-values. Fig. 4C showed the measured color components of CDs-based paper indicator. The brightness (L*-value) of the indicator fluctuated at varying pH levels, which kept almost constant when pH was at 2.0–4.0 and then showed an overall obvious downward trend when pH increased from 5.0 to 12.0. The a* value was initially negative at pH 2.0 and turned to be positive when pH was above 3.0. It showed a significant increasing trend when pH rose from pH 2.0–12.0, and reached its peak value of + 12.56 at pH 12.0. The value of b* witnessed a gradual increasing trend as the pH increased. Specifically, the b* value was kept negative in a pH range of 2.0–4.0, turn to be positive when pH was above 5.0 and reached its peak value of + 9.21 at pH 12.0. This change trend of a* and b* value at varying pH levels was consistent with the observance that the color of the CDs-based indicator changed from blue to bluish violet and finally appeared to be pink when the system shifts from acidic to alkaline environment. In addition, the overall color difference (ΔE value) of the CDs-based indicator was much greater than five in the investigated pH range (2.0–11.0), implying that the CDs-based indicator could be potentially suitable for identifying food spoilage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity to acid and ammonia vapors
ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia is a common biomarker that is used to identify food decomposition since it could simulate protein deterioration of meat products by releasing nitrogen gas (Zhai et al., 2017). To evaluate if the CDs-based indicator could be used as a gas sensor, its sensitivity to pH change was tested by observing the color change after a 30.0 min exposure to ammonia and acid vapors. As shown in the upper part of Fig. 4D, the original bright blue color of the indicator changed to vivid pink in reaction to ammonia. When it was exposed to acetic acid vapor, the indicator turned to dark blue tint. Here, the diffused ammonia/acetic acid vapors penetrated the paper, combined with water molecules to hydrolyze hydroxyl/hydrogen ions, raising or reducing pH of the indicator surface and thereafter causing color shifts. After exposing to ammonia and acid gases, the color parameters of the indicator were measured to quantify the colorimetric reaction and are shown in Fig. 4D. The a* value after exposing to ammonia increased while the value of b* after reacting with acetic acid vapor decreased, which quantitatively confirmed the observable color transitions of the CDs indicator. The CDs indicator produced ΔE values above five, verifying that the color variations can be seen by naked eyes. Since seafood release volatile amines during decomposition, the proposed CDs indicator could be potentially used as a gas-sensitive freshness indicator to track pH changes during seafood storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color stability and reproducibility of the CDs-based paper indicator
ce:section.ce:para: The color stability and reproducibility of an intelligent gas-sensitive paper indicator had crucial impacts on the results of an assay. To study color stability, the CDs-based paper indicator was stored at 25°C for one month while monitoring its chromaticity changes. The color readings of the indicator after storage at different time intervals were acquired in colorimetric mode and illustrated in Table S3. The initial color values of the indicator were (L*= 42.27, a*= –5.28 and b*= –2.93); the color values changed to (L*= 42.95, a*= –5.22, b*= –2.75, ΔE = 0.70) and (L*= 41.32, a*= –4.06, b*= –2.56, ΔE = 1.59) after storing for 20 and 30 d, respectively. The results suggested that the proposed CDs-based indicator was able to maintain a stable color at room temperature. To assess the reproducibility of the paper indicator, three independent measurements of an aqueous ammonia solution sample (20.0 mM) were carried out and the color values were recorded. As shown by Table S4, there was no apparent difference among the color values, indicating that the paper indicator was of high reproducibility, which was suitable for real seafood sample monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the CDs-based paper indicator for seafood packaging
ce:section.ce:para: Due to microbial and endogenous proteolysis, seafood spoilage would produce large amounts of BAs (Liu et al., 2018), it was reasonable that the longer the storage time, the higher the pH of seafood samples. In this regard, real applications with shrimp, fish and razor clam as samples were carried out to evaluate the practicability of the proposed gas-sensitive paper indicator. In detail, the proposed indicator was implanted on the inner side of upper lid of the storage container without direct contact with the sample ( Fig. 5), which was stored at 25 °C. To monitor the spoilage process of seafood samples, the pH and TVB-N (mg%) values were firstly acquired after storing samples at different time intervals (5 h, 12 h, 24 h and 36 h). As shown in Table 1, the initial pH of fresh shrimp, fish and razor clam was 6.56, 6.75 and 6.64, separately. During the storage period, the pH value was gradually increased and separately reached at 8.94, 9.36 and 8.39 after storage for 36 h. The initial TVB-N values of fresh shrimp, fish and razor clam was 7.00, 17.50 and 9.33 mg%. During the storage period, the TVB-N values were gradually increased and separately reached at 373.33, 645.17 and 122.50 mg% after storage for 36 h. It was seen that after being stored for 36 h, both of pH and TVB-N values of the above seafood samples exceeded the permissible upper limit for edible seafood (20 mg 100 g−1) (Ezati, Rhim, 2020), which unambiguously demonstrated the deterioration of the samples.

The sensing photo-images of the proposed indicator were captured. As shown in Fig. 5, under daylight, the color of the CDs-based indicator changed from pale blue to pink when the seafood samples were stored for 36 h (from fresh to the spoiled phase). The corresponding color values were measured after storing samples at different time intervals (5, 12, 24 and 36 h). As shown in Table 1, the color values of shrimp sample changed from (L*= 40.20, a*= –4.61, b*= –3.89) to (L*= 21.16, a*= 6.84, b*= 4.10, ΔE = 23.61) while the color values of fish and razor clam samples changed from (L*= 40.20, a*= –4.61, b*= –3.89) to (L*= 16.36, a*= 9.93, b*= 5.36, ΔE = 29.42) and to (L*= 30.62, a*= 4.96, b*= 3.74, ΔE = 15.55), respectively. Notably, after storing samples for 5, 12, 24 and 36 h, the a* values respectively increased from –4.61–6.84, 9.93 and 4.96 quantitatively confirmed the color transition from pale blue to pink. To evaluate the sample freshness, a link between TVB-N content and ΔE value was established, and the correlation coefficient was calculated by the following formula (Khan et al., 2023):
(2)
r =
∑
i = 1
n
X
i
−
X
̅
Y
i
−
Y
̅
∑
i = 1
n
X
i
−
X
̅
2
∑
i = 1
n
Y
i
−
Y
̅
2
where r, X and Y represent correlation coefficient, ΔE value and TVB-N content, respectively. High correlation coefficients of 0.95, 0.97 and 0.93 were separately obtained for shrimp, fish and razor clam samples, indicating a strong relationship between the TVB-N and the ΔE of the proposed indicator. Under UV irradiation, the color of the proposed indicator initially exhibited bright blue fluorescence, which diminished after the seafood samples were stored for 36 h and appeared to be pink color. The response of the indicator color to the seafood samples deterioration process was categorically special and can be even seen by inexperienced operators due to the large ΔE value, which confirmed the usefulness and effectiveness of the proposed gas-sensitive indicator for quick and visual monitoring of seafood freshness in a fast and non-contact manner.

In conclusion, a dual-mode gas-sensitive CDs-based paper indicator was fabricated. Under visible light, the proposed CDs-based test paper gradually changed from blue to bluish violet and then to pink with increasing pH from 2.0 to 12.0. Upon UV excitation, the blue emission intensity of the CDs-based indicator gradually diminished as pH increased and turned to be bluish violet at pH 5.0–8.0, which finally turned to pink hue at pH≥ 8.0. Importantly, the introduction of ammonia into CDs solution quenched its fluorescent intensity, displaying a good linearity with varying ammonia content in a range of 1.0–100.0 μM. The CDs-integrated paper indicator was proven to be an efficient pH-responsive detectable spoilage platform. The color of the proposed indicator changed according to the TVB-N content and pH change during the storage of seafood. The dual-mode non-contact visualization function of the proposed pH-responsive indicator could directly communicate food quality and therefore, the CDs-based smart indicator would be an efficient real-time monitor of seafood quality and freshness in the supply chain.

Hu Qin: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Gong Xiaojuan: Resources. Xu Xuechao: Methodology, Formal analysis. Xiao Lixia: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Zhou Wenyuan: Software, Resources. Guan Tianzu: Software, Resources, Formal analysis. Yang Zhen-quan: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis. Zhang Wei: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support was from LvYang Golden Phoenix Plan, Yangzhou City (2019) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: W.Z contributed to methodology, formal analysis and writing original manuscript. L.X. and Z.Y. contributed to methodology and writing–review & editing. X.X. contributed to methodology and formal analysis. T.G., W.Z., and X.G. contributed to methodology and formal analysis. Q.H. contributed to funding acquisition, supervision, methodology, formal analysis and writing–review & editing.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2025.107321.
